speaker: interviewee
text: He saved up much more too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aw, he's so cute. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is tape number four with survivor Helen Greenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, when did you arrive in Los Angeles or in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I have to tell you before Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because first, my husband started to work. He saved up-- we saved up the first few dollars. Then we-- then we borrowed some money. And my sister and her second husband, and my husband and I, we went into a luncheon at Second Avenue and 10th Street in New York, Manhattan. And by the way, my sister and I, we made it our business to start learning English. I read comic books. I read children's books. And [INAUDIBLE] said, yeah, I know you know more languages, but English is important. Thank God. God rest her soul. And we learned English in no time. And when we were in the-- in the luncheonette, and we had a lot of very intelligent people coming in-- lawyers, and judges, and teachers, and all-- and I used to learn from them. And then I started to make jokes in English, which was my-- my kind of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then if I learned a new word, I used to ask, what does it mean? And if they said that's a bad word, I said, well, what does it mean? Because if it's bad, I want to know why I shouldn't use it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked there until '53. We-- they visited a farm, and they liked the life on farm, because we worked very hard in the luncheonette. And we moved to Vineland, New Jersey, on the farm, which was a hard life, very hard. I used to go to work and supported the chicken's feet. [LAUGHTER] That's the truth. That's why-- I worked so hard that that's the time I lost a baby. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we were on the farm till '66, I think. There I go with dates. So we sold the farm, and we moved to Ventnor, which is at the outskirts of Atlantic City. My-- and I kept-- oh, while I-- I worked in different places, factories, and I wound up working the last one in Vineland at the boutique for ladies' wear. So when I came to Ventnor, my husband was driving a small-- it's a jitney. It's a mini bus. And I went to work in-- in the, again, to a boutique. That's where I was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: To California, I came-- my son came to California to visit some friends, a friend that was together with in-- in-- in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] college. They went to Drexel in Philadelphia, if you ever heard about one of the top schools. And he told them a lot of words he also used to say. Your mother looks zaftig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyhow, he-- so he moved here because his father was running a certain company, and he came here to visit. And he liked the climate. And Sam's father said, Marty, if you want to move here-- because he-- during-- may I go back a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marty went-- was going to college, so they used to have six months school, six months work. So one time there, some people were interviewed from different places. So he was interviewed from Ford Motor Company. And he was accepted to work for [INAUDIBLE] for the six months work. And he worked at the-- at the-- near-- oh, God, I can think of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, he worked for them every six months that he-- but then I said, this next six months, I said, Marty, why don't you tell them that you would like to work closer, because there was one in New Jersey, also. He said, Mom, you don't ask Ford where to work. I said, they can't-- they won't slap you. So sure enough, they did, because they-- when he worked the first six months, and they gave him a farewell party, all the people from the offices. And he still has the-- the-- the-- a radio combination with the lamp, and a card was signed by the managers, by the secretaries, and everything. That's how much they liked him. So anyhow, when he applied, and they gave him the job to work in New Jersey, so he was closer to home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he was out here in California, Sam's father said, well, if you like to move out here, I'll make out the job for you. That is, you know, it's the kind of job that you should have. And that's what he did. So every time we used to come visit, I liked the climate. And my son said, why you have to live there in the cold? He knew I don't like the cold. And all the telephone calls, because we called each other so many times a week. So we moved here. Have you had a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I moved, my sister moved, because we all move with-- together. And she lives here within walking distance, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: How have you survived all the difficulties in your life? What has been your-- Where-- from where have you drawn strength?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the upbringing that we had, a very strong upbringing. First of all, we had an upbringing is, it's better to give than to receive. And also, before you take a bite in your mouth, you give to tsedakah, which means--

speaker: interviewer
text: Charity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Charity. Also, my mom always used to say, before you say something, chew on it for 10-- count to 10. Because while you have it in you, it belongs to you. Once you let go of it, it doesn't belong to you.  And it's the same thing with doing something. So I had very smart parents, and the upbringing was out of this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you have been ill in the past.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Five years ago this month, I was declared with cancer. I beat it, just like I beat Hitler. I'm here to talk about it. And-- and my sense of humor kept me going, because I have-- if I say so myself, I have a marvelous sense of humor. I make jokes about myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't despair, and I use myself as a psychologist. I talk to myself. I pay myself. And I say, Helen, do you want to wind up on-- on drugs, or you want to wind up in a nut house? And Helen says, no, I don't. So there I cheer up, and I cheer up other people. What about your participation in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: I participate as much as possible in our synagogue. I belong to the neighborhood group. I belong to the Hadassah Group.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: B'nai Israel. Congregation B'nai Israel. I'm very happy with it. When I joined it, we had a storefront, but I liked the people. And I'm never-- I was never sorry that I joined that synagogue. I love the people, and I'm more with young people than with old people. [LAUGHS] Maybe that keeps me young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband-- we are married 49 years. In May, we'll be 50. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sorry to say that I have to watch over him a lot now, because I cannot go away. His sister used to send me money to come. She insisted to send me money for the fare, for me to come and visit with her for three weeks at a time when I moved out here. But I can't do that anymore. My sister-in-law that is in Las Vegas, I used to go out for a few days with my sister there. That's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why can't you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot do it because my husband is getting sick. Not physically. So I'm trying to be as best I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband go through the war, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was your husband born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [INAUDIBLE], Pietrokov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camps was your husband in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- he was telling me all kinds of camps. The only one that I  really know is-- [SIGHS] God-- Mauthausen and-- and-- and-- what-- I don't know.  I don't know. Now, I'm losing my mind, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. You're not-- you're fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to give you a chance to speak because I know that it's difficult for your husband to tell his story.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he can't. He wouldn't, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who survived from your husband's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: His sister, the one that lives in New York that we are very, very, very close, and a brother that lives in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to give you a chance now to introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: One, two, three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready, start.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Helen. Please introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband of 49 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Leo. And next May, we will be married 50 years. I deserve a medal, by the way. This is my son. [LAUGHTER] This is my son, Marty. And this is my--

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson, also, my pride and joy, Arthur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marty, I want to ask you a question. How has your mother's experience affected the kind of person that you are today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in several ways. I would say mostly in that it's made me more sensitive to similar type problems going on in the world today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oftentimes at work we have a lot of discussions about-- about the Holocaust and how it relates to the world today. A lot of people who aren't Jewish, or have not been involved in World War II, or not-- don't know survivors say, well, it happened then, but it can't happen again. And of course, that kind of hits my hot button, because my feeling is, well, it's happening every day. It's just that people aren't sensitive to it. So it's just a heightened sensitivity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you mother share all of her-- well, did your mother tell you what she had been through when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a burden for you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's difficult. I mean, it's not a nice story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your son? What have you shared with him? How has your experience, being the child of survivors, affected the way you bring up your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope I've been successful in conveying that sensitivity, being aware of what's going on in the world today. I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever consider hiding your Jewishness, Arthur, in a very non-Jewish world that we live in? Knowing the kind of things that your grandparents have been through, did you ever think perhaps it might be easier to just not be a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I have plenty of Jewish friends and plenty of non-Jewish friends and they don't have a problem with it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you continue your-- your Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a lot of Jewish friends and a Jewish youth group, you know. Basically, that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish youth group's about the big-- your major involvement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's the major involvement right there. I mean, you don't get much other interaction. I mean, a lot of times you go to school, you don't know someone's Jewish. But I mean, in USY, you know, everyone's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you, Helen, now, what [NON-ENGLISH] would your mother want you to share with your son and your grandson?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Let me tell you something about my son and my grandson. Well, the little one is not here. But all I can tell you is in all what I went through, through the war, and since the liberation and all, these are the blessings that I thank God a hundred times a day. My son was the easiest to raise. Thank God had never problems with him. And it's not just because I say so now. But he was-- my neighbors and my friends always used to say, when there was-- they started to complain about their children, [LAUGHS] they used-- they used to say, you don't talk. You don't have anything what to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He was.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [NON-ENGLISH]. He still is [NON-ENGLISH]. I'll tell you something else. He is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what about his son?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is-- well, I'll get to him. He is the son and daughter-- the daughter that I almost had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, is this kid [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: This kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This kid is, when we go someplace out, and he'll hold onto his zaide. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And when I showed him one time a picture of somebody that I met in-- in Berlin, that we-- his uncle was a cantor, and he wanted me very badly to date him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Arthur says to me one time, if you remember that, he says, I'm glad you didn't marry him. I'm glad you married zaide. Do you remember that, sweetie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to wish you all now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Very good health, to tell you it's an honor to be here with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You were a--

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to wish you good health. Grow strong in your Judaism, and continue on the legacy, Arthur, of your grandparents and your great-grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you are really an inspiration. I'll be honest to you. It's not because you were a [INAUDIBLE], but you're a nice person. And those guys are nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my mother and father. This is an enlargement from an original picture that was sent 1938 to the United States. And I made an enlargement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Devora Leah, and my father's name, Mattis Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name Fromer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warsaw, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, Poland. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the occasion was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, they-- in the original picture, there is in Hebrew and in Polish, wishing a Happy New Year for the high holidays. Why you're a few minutes late?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mom and dad. I was in some kind of a-- I was hurt sometime there, and they wanted to make me feel better. And they took a picture with me. That's my mom and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's myself. This is Devora Leah Fromer, Mattis Shlomo Fromer, and [? Nahama ?] Fromer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately either-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: He'll tell you when to start. Watch Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so anxious to talk

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if you can get the 25 in the flower bouquet. That was the silver anniversary of my parents, Devor-- Devora Leah Fromer, Mattis Shlomo Fromer. It was a huge party for their 25th wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the child standing behind your mother to her right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not children. They were grownups.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of the people in the picture behind them? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: One is my brother, Leo Fromer. He is the one that was in the United States. The other one is just friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken? Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my God, certainly. I was a young school kid when that was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Warsaw, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was all Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother. That was not his profession, being-- he was amateur photographer. So he took a picture of himself and my mom. And [? Edic ?] Fromer, mom, Devora Leah Fromer. He put the camera on a timer, and they're sitting in front of a mirror, if you can see that-- the reflection in the mirror of the two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- in the-- in the '30s. Exactly, I couldn't pinpoint.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything happened in the '30s, in the Warsaw, because I can't pinpoint. Till '39, everything was normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your mother? You have to describe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the corner of the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Devora Leah Fromer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Devora Leah Fromer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your parents' home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my-- in our home. This is a picture of my youngest brother and his wife. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture on the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have to use--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please show us, who is the person that--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that hid your brother. Which one was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The person in the top left corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the right of the photograph. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yannick. ?] I don't remember last names.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish. He spoke a better Yiddish than I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother did he hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one-- [? Edic. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This was taken in Warsaw, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our home, yeah. And this is my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: The father is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mattis Shlomo

speaker: interviewer
text: --standing on the right side, the third one from the right. Are you in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: You took-- I don't want any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone particularly famous in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this one, next door neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my sister Rosie, so I must be-- oh, and I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Helen is standing in the center to the-- to the left of the woman who's seated at the end of the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people over? [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. I'll count them. One, two, three, four, the fifth person.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember them all, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: The main thing is the family pic. Time you get done with her, you'll learn Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- June-- June 7 was my bir-- my 16th birthday. At that time, we never bought ready clothes, by the way. Everything was made to order. So I got my first grownup outfit with a little head of the face. And-- and this is my-- the 16th birthday on a side street that my brother took. This my brother took, that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, on a side street.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, figure out. No, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, June 7, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at that. What happened to me? Oh, God. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I saw this picture, or-- or whatever you call, during a 1978, '79 when they were showing from Holocaust, a movie on television. I saw my mom's-- I recognized my mother there. And I went to New York to find the-- the-- the-- NBC-- NBC borrowed those pictures that the Nazis took. And-- and there are three younger women, and the one that is older, you can see. It-- she survived with-- she's surrounded-- excuse me-- she's surrounded with dead bodies and soldiers. And they were the next to be exterminated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Devora Leah Fromer, Helen's mother, is the second one from the left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the shortest woman in the picture. How old was she in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Who knows how long they were schlepping her around?

speaker: interviewer
text: She was in her late 50s?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was taken-- when she was taken away. So maybe they got her into the 60s, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which camp this was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because you didn't wind up in your own cities, or your own places of birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and myself, Leo Greenbaum, Helen Greenbaum, and in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- well, figure out. My-- my-- my son was three-- three months in the tummy.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Yeah. '46. No, '40-- '46, yes. Yes, I'm  sorry. You were born-- yes, you were born '47. I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wasn't bad looking, your father, you know something?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was about three. Arthur's about eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Martin Greenbaum, my grandson, Arthur Greenbaum, and my younger grandson, Ian Greenbaurm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cali-- what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here in Irvine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irvine, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are the children today in 1996?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is 18, just turned 18. And another one is 12. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and-- and I brought--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what you call a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I brought this one that it survived in Czechoslovakia. And I brought this, and that also survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You brought a [INAUDIBLE] from Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Shh, shh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister and I, Rosalie Wattenberg, and Helen Greenbaum, myself, are showing a table cloth that survived the war in-- in the United States, because my mother used to embroider. That was her hobby. And she sent this table cloth to my Aunt, [? Rae. ?] And there are two initials on it, my-- my mom's initial and my Aunt's [? Rea's ?] initial. And I cherish that table cloth. Hopefully, my-- my son will cherish it as much one day when he's going to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, he's going to get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's going to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK. That's OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody made-- this was not a bought drawing. Somebody did draw the-- my-- my-- about the-- a wish for Shabbat. And-- and they made a herring, and a fish, and a bottle of wine, and a challah and my mom's name, Devora Leah Fromer, embroidered there. And that was sent to the United States, also, to my-- to my Aunt [? Rae. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was sent before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: All before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Warsaw to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
